## How GPT Works

At a high level, GPT is a text-in, text-out machine.
The interesting part is the route between those two pieces of text:

![The GPT data path: text becomes tokens, vectors, contextual vectors, token probabilities, and then text again](images/fig-ch01-gpt-overview.png){#fig-ch01-gpt-overview}

When you type a prompt, GPT does not understand the raw characters directly.
It first turns the text into token IDs, turns those IDs into vectors, passes the vectors through a stack of transformer blocks, then predicts the most likely next token.
That new token is appended to the prompt, and the same process runs again.
Long answers are built by repeating this loop one token at a time.

![A folded example of the GPT pipeline: prompt text moves through tokenization and embeddings, then down into transformer blocks, projection, probabilities, and a sampled next token](images/fig-ch01-gpt-pipeline.png){#fig-ch01-gpt-pipeline}

The pipeline has four main stages.

*1. Tokenize the text (@sec-ch03-tokens).*

Text is split into small pieces called *tokens*.
Tokens are usually subword chunks, not full words and not single characters.
For example, `"unhappiness"` might become something like `"un"` + `"happiness"`, or a different pair of chunks depending on the tokenizer's learned vocabulary.

The tokenizer then replaces each token with an integer ID.
From this point onward, the model works with numbers, not strings.

*2. Turn token IDs into vectors (@sec-ch04-embeddings and @sec-ch05-positional-encoding).*

A token ID such as `428` is only a lookup key.
The embedding layer uses that key to fetch a learned vector: a list of numbers that places the token in the model's internal space.
Tokens used in similar contexts tend to end up with related vectors.

GPT also needs word order.
The sequence `"dog bites man"` should not look the same as `"man bites dog"`.
Position information is therefore added to the token representation, either before attention as positional encoding (@sec-ch05-positional-encoding) or inside attention itself in modern models such as RoPE (@sec-ch07-rope).

*3. Process the context with transformer blocks (@sec-ch06-attention through @sec-ch10-transformer-block).*

The vectors now pass through a stack of transformer blocks.
This is where each token's representation becomes contextual: the vector for a token is updated using information from the other tokens around it.

Inside each block, attention decides which earlier tokens matter for the current position.
In `"I kicked the ball and it bounced"`, attention gives the model a way to connect `"it"` with `"ball"`.
Multi-head attention runs several of these relationship checks at once (@sec-ch08-multi-head-attention).
The feed-forward network then transforms each position's vector independently, adding another learned computation step (@sec-ch09-feed-forward).
Residual connections and layer normalization keep this deep stack trainable (@sec-ch10-transformer-block).

*4. Predict and decode the next token (@sec-ch11-vocab-projection).*

After the final transformer block, GPT has a contextual vector for each position.
For generation, the important one is the final position: it represents the prompt as seen from the point where the next token should appear.

The vocabulary projection turns that vector into one score per vocabulary token.
These scores are called *logits*.
A softmax converts logits into probabilities, and a decoding rule chooses the next token.
Sometimes the highest-probability token is chosen; often the model samples from likely candidates so the answer is not always identical.

Then the selected token is converted back into text, appended to the prompt, and fed through the model again.
That repeated next-token prediction loop is what produces paragraphs, code blocks, summaries, and conversations.

::: {.callout-note}
### Where training fits

The pipeline above describes a forward pass: how GPT produces one next-token prediction.
Training uses the same forward pass, but adds two extra steps.
First, the loss function measures how far the prediction was from the real next token (@sec-ch12-loss).
Then backpropagation adjusts the model's weights so the next prediction is a little better (@sec-ch13-training).

At chat time, the weights are already learned.
The model is running inference: tokenizing your prompt, computing vectors, predicting the next token, and repeating.
:::

The rest of this book expands each box in the pipeline.
We start with the notation needed to read the math (@sec-ch02-notation), then build the path from tokens to embeddings, attention, transformer blocks, vocabulary projection, loss, and training.
